refined_fr.refined_fr: Enable measurement and reporting of energy consumption per unit of work output to support validation against customer-defined energy efficiency targets.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) the system's capability to measure/report energy metrics, and (2) the achievement of a performance target. The refined FR isolates the system's functional responsibility (instrumentation and data provision) from the acceptance criterion (target satisfaction), which is a verification gate, not a functional requirement. This keeps the FR solution-neutral by not prescribing how efficiency is achieved (e.g., via motor selection, load optimization, route planning, or operational procedures), only that the system provides the data needed to assess it.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Verify that the NGHE solution provides real-time and post-mission energy consumption data (kWh or equivalent) and work output metrics (tons moved, distance, cycles) with sufficient accuracy and granularity to compute energy_per_ton = energy_used / ton_moved. Confirm that reported values are traceable to calibrated sensors and that data is accessible to Site Manager and System Engineer for compliance assessment.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-003.1: Measure and log total energy consumed by the NGHE solution during operation.
FR-NGHE-003.2: Measure and log work output (e.g., mass moved, distance traveled, or equivalent unit) during operation.
FR-NGHE-003.3: Compute and report the energy efficiency metric (energy per unit work output) in a form accessible to authorized stakeholders.
FR-NGHE-003.4: Provide traceability and audit trail for energy and work output measurements to support acceptance verification.

candidate_dps: DP1: Decompose energy consumption measurement into (a) instantaneous power acquisition from energy source, (b) continuous integration over evaluation period, and (c) independent work output quantification; correlate via post-processing to derive energy_per_ton ratio.

DP2: Embed energy metering at the primary energy source boundary (e.g., fuel tank, electrical supply point) with synchronized work-output counters (e.g., tonnage sensors on load path); compute ratio in real-time or batch mode.

DP3: Instrument the system with distributed energy sensors (motor current, fuel flow, hydraulic pressure) feeding a centralized data aggregation module that normalizes heterogeneous signals and correlates with work-output telemetry to compute efficiency metrics.

DP4: Establish a measurement architecture with three independent logical channels: (i) energy source metering, (ii) work-output quantification, (iii) time-synchronized data fusion and ratio computation; each channel operates autonomously with well-defined handoff interfaces.

DP5: Implement energy efficiency validation as a closed-loop feedback system: measure energy and work output continuously, compute energy_per_ton in real-time, compare against E_target threshold, and trigger operational alerts or corrective actions if threshold is exceeded.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP4: Establish a measurement architecture with three independent logical channels: (i) energy source metering, (ii) work-output quantification, (iii) time-synchronized data fusion and ratio computation; each channel operates autonomously with well-defined handoff interfaces.

potential_sources: Time synchronization drift between energy and work-output channels could introduce systematic bias in ratio computation; mitigated by explicit synchronization protocol at data fusion boundary.
Energy source metering and work-output quantification may operate at different sampling rates or latencies; decoupled via buffering and alignment logic in fusion module.

Dependency on external worksite operational policies (e.g., duty cycle, load profiles) could affect measurement validity; addressed by explicit assumption documentation and evaluation period definition.
Heterogeneous energy sources (fuel, electrical, hybrid) require normalized metering; DP4 supports this via abstraction at fusion layer without prescribing source-specific instrumentation.
coupling_check.notes: DP4 achieves low coupling by separating concerns: energy metering is independent of work-output sensing, and both feed into a neutral fusion layer. This allows substitution of energy sources or work-output sensors without redesign of the overall measurement logic. The three-channel architecture supports modularity and testability.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Work output (tonnage moved) can be quantified with sufficient accuracy and temporal resolution to correlate with energy consumption over the evaluation period.
ASSUMPTION: Energy source boundary is well-defined and accessible for metering (e.g., fuel tank inlet/outlet, electrical supply terminals).
ASSUMPTION: Evaluation period is long enough and operationally representative to yield statistically valid energy_per_ton estimates; short or atypical duty cycles may not reflect true efficiency.

RISK: Measurement instrumentation introduces parasitic energy consumption (e.g., sensor power draw, data logging overhead) that could bias energy_per_ton downward; mitigation: quantify and document sensor overhead as a known correction factor.
RISK: Time synchronization between distributed channels may drift or fail; mitigation: implement redundant synchronization (e.g., GPS, local clock cross-check) and alert on sync loss.

RISK: Customer-defined E_target may be based on idealized conditions (lab testing) that do not match field worksite conditions (ambient temperature, load variability, operator skill); mitigation: establish baseline measurement protocol and document operational context.

RISK: Data fusion and ratio computation logic may contain algorithmic errors (e.g., division by zero if work output is zero, unit conversion mistakes); mitigation: implement validation checks and unit-aware computation framework.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP4 to explicitly specify: (1) the logical interface contract between energy metering and fusion (e.g., energy samples with timestamp and unit), (2) the logical interface contract between work-output quantification and fusion (e.g., tonnage increments with timestamp), (3) the synchronization protocol (e.g., wall-clock alignment, buffering strategy for out-of-order samples), and (4) the ratio computation algorithm (e.g., cumulative energy / cumulative tonnage over sliding or fixed windows). This will support clear IM (Implementation Module) boundaries and enable independent testing of each channel. Additionally, add a fourth logical channel for measurement validation and anomaly detection (e.g., sanity checks on energy_per_ton range, detection of zero-work periods) to reduce risk of silent measurement failures.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Centralized measurement architecture encompassing energy source metering, work-output quantification, time-synchronized data fusion, and efficiency ratio computation with three independent logical channels and autonomous operation.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Exports computed efficiency ratios, energy metrics, and work-output quantification to external monitoring and logging systems.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operational directives and measurement configuration parameters from Vehicle or Teleoperation control layers.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Synchronizes time-reference signals across distributed measurement channels and exchanges calibration/configuration data with remote systems.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent containment: NGHE Solution is explicitly designed for energy-efficiency measurement and analysis. All three logical channels (energy metering, work quantification, data fusion) align with core NGHE responsibilities. Minimal diffusion: only Telemetry export and Tasking input cross boundaries; time synchronization via Network Link is internal to measurement architecture.

Poor containment: Vehicle module typically owns propulsion, steering, and mechanical systems. Measurement architecture is a cross-cutting concern that would fragment across energy source (engine/battery), work-output sensors (drivetrain), and timing logic. High diffusion: measurement logic would scatter across multiple Vehicle subsystems, violating cohesion.

Very poor containment: Teleoperation is a command-and-control interface layer, not a measurement platform. Placing measurement architecture here creates semantic mismatch and forces teleoperation to own low-level sensor fusion and ratio computation. High diffusion: energy and work-output data originate in Vehicle; fusion logic would be orphaned from data sources.

Poor containment: Safety Services owns hazard detection and SafetyAlert generation. While efficiency metrics may inform safety decisions, the measurement architecture itself is not a safety function. Placing it here conflates measurement with safety logic. High diffusion: energy and work-output channels would need to reach back to Vehicle; fusion logic would be misaligned with safety-specific responsibilities.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The NGHE Solution module is the natural owner of the measurement architecture because: (1) it is explicitly chartered for energy-efficiency analysis and reporting; (2) all three logical channels (energy metering, work quantification, data fusion) are core to NGHE's functional scope; (3) the three-channel design with autonomous operation and handoff interfaces aligns with NGHE's role as a dedicated measurement and analysis subsystem; (4) external interfaces (Telemetry export, Tasking input, Network Link synchronization) are standard NGHE integration points; (5) containment avoids scattering measurement logic across Vehicle, Teleoperation, or Safety Services, each of which has distinct primary responsibilities.
containment_risks: Risk: If energy source metering requires direct access to Vehicle propulsion subsystems (e.g., engine ECU, battery management), the boundary between NGHE Solution and Vehicle may become blurred. Mitigation: Define a clean Tasking or Telemetry interface through which Vehicle exposes energy-source data without NGHE owning the sensor hardware.

Risk: If work-output quantification depends on real-time Vehicle drivetrain signals (torque, speed), latency or synchronization issues may arise if NGHE Solution is physically distant from Vehicle. Mitigation: Ensure Network Link provides deterministic time-sync and that work-output channel operates with acceptable latency bounds.

Risk: If Safety Services needs to react to efficiency anomalies (e.g., energy spike indicating fault), NGHE Solution must export SafetyAlert-compatible signals or Safety Services must subscribe to Telemetry. Mitigation: Define explicit SafetyAlert handoff interface if efficiency metrics inform safety decisions.

Risk: Three independent logical channels may introduce data consistency or ordering issues if not carefully synchronized. Mitigation: Ensure time-synchronized data fusion channel is robust and that handoff interfaces between channels are well-specified.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP4 directly addresses the FR by establishing a three-channel measurement architecture (energy source metering, work-output quantification, time-synchronized data fusion) that enables computation of energy_per_unit_work ratios. The logical decomposition into autonomous channels with well-defined handoff interfaces is sound and supports validation against customer-defined energy efficiency targets (E_target). The DP is sufficiently detailed to guide implementation and is traceable to the acceptance criterion: measured energy_per_ton ≤ E_target.

diffusion_risks: Energy source metering channel: Risk of boundary blur if NGHE Solution requires direct ownership of Vehicle propulsion subsystem sensors (engine ECU, battery management). Mitigation: Enforce clean Tasking interface through which Vehicle exposes energy-source telemetry without NGHE owning sensor hardware.

Work-output quantification channel: Risk of latency/synchronization degradation if real-time Vehicle drivetrain signals (torque, speed) are accessed across physical distance or through lossy interfaces. Mitigation: Validate Network Link provides deterministic time-sync and measure end-to-end latency against acceptable bounds for efficiency ratio accuracy.

Data fusion and ratio computation: Risk of data consistency or ordering anomalies if the three channels are not tightly synchronized. Mitigation: Specify time-synchronized data fusion channel robustness requirements and formalize handoff interface contracts (e.g., timestamp alignment, ordering guarantees).

Safety Services integration: Risk of semantic mismatch if Safety Services needs to react to efficiency anomalies (e.g., energy spike as fault indicator). Mitigation: Define explicit SafetyAlert export interface from NGHE Solution or establish subscription mechanism for Safety Services to consume Telemetry efficiency metrics.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution achieves 0.92 containment score with 0.08 diffusion. All three logical channels (energy metering, work quantification, data fusion) are cohesively owned within NGHE's chartered scope as a dedicated measurement and analysis subsystem. External interfaces (Telemetry export, Tasking input, Network Link synchronization) are standard integration points that do not violate module boundaries. Alternative candidates (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) exhibit poor containment (0.30–0.45) and high diffusion (0.55–0.70) due to semantic mismatch and scattering of measurement logic across unrelated responsibilities. NGHE Solution is the natural and justified owner.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. The NGHE Solution module boundary is explicit and well-scoped. Acceptance criterion (energy_per_ton ≤ E_target) is directly computable from outputs of the three-channel architecture: energy_used from energy source metering, ton_moved from work-output quantification, and ratio computed by data fusion. Telemetry interface exports computed efficiency ratios, enabling external validation against customer-defined targets. Measurement architecture is self-contained and does not depend on external modules for core computation logic.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Clear. FR is allocated to DP4 (three-channel measurement architecture), which is realized by NGHE Solution module. Ownership boundaries are unambiguous: NGHE Solution owns measurement logic and data fusion; Vehicle owns propulsion/drivetrain hardware and exposes energy/work-output data via Tasking interface; Teleoperation owns command-and-control; Safety Services owns hazard detection. Interfaces are well-defined (Telemetry out, Tasking in, Network Link inout). No allocation ambiguity or orphaned responsibilities detected.

recommended_revisions: Formalize the Tasking interface contract to specify: (1) energy-source data schema (units, precision, timestamp format), (2) work-output data schema (units, precision, timestamp format), (3) latency and synchronization requirements for time-reference signals via Network Link. This ensures Vehicle and NGHE Solution maintain clean boundaries.

Define time-synchronized data fusion channel specification: (1) timestamp alignment tolerance across the three channels, (2) ordering guarantees for energy and work-output samples, (3) handling of out-of-order or missing data. This mitigates data consistency risks.

Establish SafetyAlert export interface or subscription mechanism if Safety Services must react to efficiency anomalies. Specify which efficiency metrics (e.g., energy spike, ratio deviation) trigger safety-relevant signals and the latency requirements for safety response.

Document Network Link synchronization protocol: (1) time-reference signal distribution mechanism, (2) clock drift tolerance, (3) calibration/configuration data exchange format. This ensures deterministic time-sync across distributed measurement channels.

Add acceptance criterion refinement: specify measurement accuracy requirements (e.g., ±5% energy measurement error, ±3% work-output quantification error) to ensure energy_per_ton ratio meets customer validation confidence levels.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass with high confidence. The three-channel measurement architecture is logically sound, well-contained within NGHE Solution, and verification is feasible. However, the recommended_revisions identify critical interface contracts and synchronization specifications that are not yet formalized at the current level. Given high safety criticality (energy efficiency anomalies may indicate propulsion faults), decomposing the three logical channels into explicit sub-FRs will: (1) force formalization of Tasking interface contracts (energy-source and work-output data schemas, latency bounds), (2) make time-synchronized data fusion robustness requirements explicit and traceable, (3) enable safety-relevant efficiency anomaly detection to be designed with clear ownership and latency guarantees. Proceeding to next-level decomposition ensures that interface contracts and safety-critical synchronization logic are not deferred to implementation, where they risk being overlooked or inconsistently realized.
next_level_sub_frs: FR4.1: Acquire energy-source telemetry (engine ECU fuel consumption, battery state-of-charge, energy draw) from Vehicle propulsion subsystem with specified schema, precision, and timestamp alignment via Tasking interface.

FR4.2: Quantify work-output (ton-kilometers moved, payload mass, distance traveled) from Vehicle drivetrain signals (torque, speed, position) with specified schema, precision, and timestamp alignment via Tasking interface.

FR4.3: Synchronize energy-source and work-output data streams across three measurement channels using time-reference signals from Network Link with specified clock drift tolerance and ordering guarantees.

FR4.4: Compute energy-per-unit-work ratio (energy_per_ton) from synchronized energy and work-output samples and validate against customer-defined target (E_target ≤ threshold) with specified measurement accuracy and confidence bounds.

FR4.5: Export computed efficiency ratios and anomaly indicators (energy spikes, ratio deviations) via Telemetry interface to enable Safety Services to detect propulsion faults and trigger safety-relevant alerts with specified latency.

revise.why: No revisions required at current level. FR4 and DP4 are sufficiently clear and well-justified. Recommended_revisions are appropriately addressed by next-level decomposition, not by reworking the current level.

assumptions: Vehicle Tasking interface can expose energy-source telemetry (fuel consumption, battery state-of-charge) and work-output signals (torque, speed, position) without requiring NGHE Solution to own propulsion hardware.
Network Link provides deterministic time-synchronization service with clock drift tolerance suitable for efficiency ratio accuracy (assumed ±5% measurement error acceptable).
Safety Services can subscribe to or poll efficiency anomaly indicators from NGHE Solution Telemetry interface without requiring bidirectional control coupling.
Customer-defined energy efficiency target (E_target) is stable and measurable in units of energy per unit work (e.g., kWh/ton or MJ/ton).

Three-channel architecture (energy metering, work quantification, data fusion) is sufficient to capture all relevant energy flows and work outputs; no hidden energy sinks or work-output modes are present.
open_risks: Tasking interface contract (energy-source and work-output data schemas, latency, timestamp format) is not yet formalized. Risk: Vehicle and NGHE Solution may implement incompatible data formats or synchronization assumptions, causing measurement errors or data loss. Mitigation: Formalize Tasking interface specification before detailed design.

Time-synchronized data fusion channel robustness is not yet specified. Risk: Clock drift, out-of-order samples, or missing data may corrupt efficiency ratio computation, leading to false anomaly detection or missed faults. Mitigation: Define timestamp alignment tolerance, ordering guarantees, and missing-data handling in FR4.3 decomposition.

Safety-relevant efficiency anomaly detection (FR4.5) lacks explicit latency and confidence requirements. Risk: Safety Services may not react to propulsion faults in time, or false positives may trigger unnecessary safety interventions. Mitigation: Establish SafetyAlert export interface contract with latency and false-positive rate bounds in FR4.5 decomposition.

Measurement accuracy requirements (±5% energy, ±3% work-output) are recommended but not yet formalized. Risk: Efficiency ratio may not meet customer validation confidence levels. Mitigation: Refine acceptance criterion in FR4.4 decomposition to specify measurement accuracy and confidence bounds.

Network Link synchronization protocol (clock distribution, drift tolerance, calibration) is assumed but not yet detailed. Risk: Distributed measurement channels may experience clock skew, causing timestamp misalignment and efficiency ratio errors. Mitigation: Formalize Network Link synchronization specification in FR4.3 decomposition.
evidence_needed: Tasking interface specification: energy-source data schema (units, precision, timestamp format, sampling rate), work-output data schema (units, precision, timestamp format, sampling rate), latency bounds for data delivery, synchronization requirements for time-reference signals.

Network Link synchronization protocol: time-reference signal distribution mechanism, clock drift tolerance (e.g., ±10 ppm), calibration/configuration data exchange format, end-to-end latency measurement across distributed channels.

Time-synchronized data fusion channel specification: timestamp alignment tolerance (e.g., ±10 ms), ordering guarantees for energy and work-output samples, handling of out-of-order or missing data (e.g., buffering, interpolation, error flags).

Safety-relevant efficiency anomaly detection specification: which efficiency metrics trigger safety alerts (e.g., energy spike >20%, ratio deviation >15%), latency requirements for safety response (e.g., <100 ms), false-positive rate bounds (e.g., <1 per 1000 hours).

Measurement accuracy validation: ±5% energy measurement error, ±3% work-output quantification error, confidence level (e.g., 95th percentile), traceability to customer-defined E_target validation requirements.

Vehicle Tasking interface implementation feasibility: confirmation that Vehicle propulsion subsystem (engine ECU, battery management, drivetrain sensors) can expose required telemetry without requiring NGHE Solution to own sensor hardware or control logic.
